questions.questionText: Glucose exists predominantly as a cyclic hemiacetal in solution. The clinically relevant difference between α-D-glucopyranose and β-D-glucopyranose is:
questions.optionA: The orientation of the OH on the anomeric carbon (C1) — α (axial/below) vs β (equatorial/above); this determines which enzymes can cleave glycosidic bonds (e.g., humans have α-glucosidase but not β-glucosidase for cellulose)
questions.optionB: One is a reducing sugar and the other is not
questions.optionC: They have different optical rotations only
questions.explanation: Anomers and clinical significance: α and β anomers differ ONLY at C1 (anomeric carbon) — OH below the ring plane (α) or above (β). In solution: mutarotation equilibrium (~36% α, ~64% β at equilibrium). Clinical relevance: ENZYME SPECIFICITY depends on anomeric configuration. Glycogen/starch: α(1→4) and α(1→6) bonds → cleaved by α-amylase, maltase. Cellulose: β(1→4) bonds → requires cellulase (humans LACK this → cannot digest cellulose/fiber). Lactose: β(1→4) Gal-Glc → requires β-galactosidase (lactase). Lactase deficiency → lactose intolerance. This is why dietary fiber (cellulose) passes through undigested despite being made of glucose — same monomer, different linkage. Reference: Harper's — Anomers.

questions.questionText: Sucrose is the most commonly consumed disaccharide worldwide. Its rapid hydrolysis in the gut and metabolic consequences differentiate it from other sugars because:
questions.optionB: It produces only glucose on hydrolysis
questions.optionC: It requires no enzyme for digestion
questions.optionD: Sucrase (α-glucosidase) at the intestinal brush border rapidly hydrolyzes sucrose → glucose + fructose; fructose enters hepatic metabolism via fructokinase → F-1-P → bypasses PFK-1 regulation → unrestricted glycolysis and lipogenesis, contributing to metabolic syndrome
questions.explanation: Sucrose metabolism — NAFLD/metabolic syndrome link: Sucrose → glucose + FRUCTOSE (by sucrase). Fructose metabolism (liver-specific, ~70% of dietary fructose): Fructokinase → fructose-1-phosphate (F-1-P, uses ATP → transient phosphate/ATP depletion → uric acid generation). Aldolase B → DHAP + glyceraldehyde → enter glycolysis/gluconeogenesis. Critical issue: fructose metabolism BYPASSES PFK-1 (the rate-limiting, regulated step of glycolysis) → unregulated carbon flux → excess acetyl-CoA → de novo lipogenesis → TG accumulation → NAFLD, VLDL overproduction → dyslipidemia. Also: no insulin stimulation from fructose (no satiety) → overconsumption. This explains the link between high-fructose corn syrup and metabolic syndrome. Reference: Harper's — Fructose Metabolism.
questions.conceptTag: Fructose PFK-1 bypass lipogenesis metabolic syndrome

questions.questionText: Glycogen storage differs between liver and muscle. The structural feature of glycogen that enables rapid glucose mobilization is:
questions.optionA: Extensive branching (every 8-12 residues) creates numerous non-reducing ends — each end is a substrate for glycogen phosphorylase, enabling simultaneous rapid release of multiple glucose-1-phosphate molecules
questions.explanation: Glycogen structure and rapid mobilization: Glycogen: α(1→4) linear chains + α(1→6) branch points every 8-12 glucose residues. Highly branched → HUGE number of NON-REDUCING ENDS (outermost glucose on each branch). Glycogen phosphorylase acts ONLY at non-reducing ends (cleaves α-1,4 bonds sequentially until 4 residues from branch point). More branches = more non-reducing ends = more simultaneous phosphorylase molecules can work = FASTER glucose mobilization. Glycogen particle: up to 55,000 glucose residues, 12 tiers of branching, MW ~10⁷ Da. Liver glycogen: ~100g (whole-body glucose homeostasis), has G6Pase → releases free glucose. Muscle glycogen: ~400g (local fuel only), NO G6Pase → G6P trapped → enters glycolysis. Glycogenin: self-glucosylating primer protein at center. Reference: Harper's — Glycogen Structure.
questions.conceptTag: Glycogen branching non-reducing ends rapid mobilization

questions.questionText: A reducing sugar is one that has:
questions.optionD: A free anomeric carbon (can reduce Cu2+ in Benedict's/Fehling's test)
questions.explanation: Reducing sugars have a free anomeric carbon (C1 in aldoses, C2 in ketoses) that can exist in open-chain form and reduce oxidizing agents (Cu2+ → Cu+ in Benedict's reagent → brick-red precipitate). All monosaccharides are reducing sugars. Disaccharides: maltose, lactose are reducing; sucrose is NON-reducing (both anomeric carbons are bonded). Clinically, Benedict's test detects glucose in urine. Reference: Harper's — Sugar Properties.

questions.questionText: Glycosaminoglycans (GAGs) are composed of repeating disaccharide units containing:
questions.optionA: An amino sugar and a uronic acid
questions.explanation: GAGs: linear polymers of repeating disaccharide units — one amino sugar (N-acetylglucosamine or N-acetylgalactosamine) + one uronic acid (glucuronic acid or iduronic acid). Highly negatively charged (sulfate groups + carboxyl groups) → attract water → gel-like matrix. Examples: hyaluronic acid (only unsulfated GAG), chondroitin sulfate (cartilage), heparan sulfate, keratan sulfate, dermatan sulfate. Attached to core proteins → proteoglycans. Reference: Harper's — GAGs.

questions.questionText: Galactose is converted to glucose by the enzyme pathway involving:
questions.explanation: Leloir pathway: Galactose → (galactokinase) → Gal-1-P → (Gal-1-P uridylyltransferase using UDP-glucose) → Glc-1-P + UDP-Gal → (UDP-Gal 4-epimerase) → UDP-Glc (recycled). Classic galactosemia: deficiency of Gal-1-P uridylyltransferase → Gal-1-P accumulation → cataracts, liver damage, intellectual disability. Non-classic: galactokinase deficiency → cataracts only. Reference: Harper's — Galactose Metabolism.

questions.questionText: The glycosidic bond in lactose is:
questions.explanation: Lactose: galactose β1→4 glucose. It is a reducing disaccharide (glucose has free anomeric C1). Found exclusively in milk. Lactase (β-galactosidase) in intestinal brush border cleaves it. Lactose intolerance: lactase deficiency → undigested lactose → colonic bacterial fermentation → gas, bloating, osmotic diarrhea. Lactose synthesis in mammary gland requires lactose synthase (galactosyltransferase + α-lactalbumin). Reference: Harper's — Lactose.

questions.questionText: Mucopolysaccharidoses (MPS) are caused by deficiency of:
questions.explanation: MPS: group of lysosomal storage diseases caused by deficiency of specific lysosomal hydrolases that degrade GAGs → accumulation of undegraded GAGs in tissues. Examples: Hurler syndrome (MPS IH — α-L-iduronidase deficiency, dermatan/heparan sulfate), Hunter syndrome (MPS II — iduronate sulfatase, X-linked), Sanfilippo (MPS III — heparan sulfate). Features: coarse facies, skeletal abnormalities, hepatosplenomegaly, intellectual disability. Reference: Harper's — MPS.

questions.questionText: Glycoproteins differ from proteoglycans in that glycoproteins have:
questions.optionA: Short, branched oligosaccharide chains attached to protein
questions.explanation: Glycoproteins: proteins with short, branched oligosaccharide chains (N-linked to asparagine or O-linked to serine/threonine). Most plasma proteins are glycoproteins (except albumin). Proteoglycans: core protein with one or more long, linear GAG chains — mostly carbohydrate by weight. Blood group antigens (ABO) are glycoproteins/glycolipids. N-glycosylation occurs in ER (dolichol phosphate pathway), O-glycosylation in Golgi. Reference: Harper's — Glycoproteins.

questions.questionText: Hyaluronic acid differs from other GAGs in that it:
questions.optionD: Is the only GAG that is unsulfated and not covalently linked to protein
questions.explanation: Hyaluronic acid (hyaluronan): unique among GAGs — NOT sulfated, NOT covalently linked to a core protein, very large (up to millions of Da), composed of repeating GlcA-GlcNAc disaccharides. Found abundantly in synovial fluid (lubricant), vitreous humor, connective tissue. Hyaluronidase (in sperm acrosome, some bacteria) degrades it — 'spreading factor.' Used therapeutically: joint injections (viscosupplementation), dermal fillers. Reference: Harper's — Hyaluronic Acid.

questions.questionText: Epimers differ from each other in configuration at:
questions.explanation: Epimers differ at ONE specific chiral carbon (not the anomeric carbon — those are anomers). Glucose and galactose are C4 epimers. Glucose and mannose are C2 epimers. Anomers: differ at C1 (α and β forms). Enantiomers: mirror images at ALL chiral centers (D and L forms). Diastereomers: differ at more than one but not all carbons. Interconversion: UDP-galactose 4-epimerase converts Gal ↔ Glc. Reference: Harper's — Sugar Isomers.

questions.questionText: 2-deoxyribose lacks the hydroxyl group at C2 of ribose. This structural modification is critical for DNA because:
questions.optionA: It allows DNA to form single strands
questions.optionD: The absence of 2'-OH makes the phosphodiester backbone resistant to alkaline hydrolysis (unlike RNA) and prevents 2'-OH-mediated self-cleavage, ensuring DNA's role as a stable long-term genetic information store
questions.explanation: 2'-deoxyribose → DNA stability: RNA has 2'-OH → acts as nucleophile → attacks adjacent phosphodiester bond → cleaves RNA chain (especially in alkaline conditions or via ribonuclease). This is why: (1) RNA is chemically LABILE (self-hydrolysis), (2) Ribonuclease needs only the 2'-OH for catalysis. DNA LACKS 2'-OH → no nucleophilic attack → phosphodiester backbone is chemically STABLE → can persist for thousands of years (ancient DNA). This structural difference explains the fundamental division: DNA = stable permanent storage, RNA = transient information carrier. Ribonucleotide reductase: converts ribonucleotides to deoxyribonucleotides (replaces 2'-OH with 2'-H via radical mechanism) — this is the enzyme that 'invented' DNA. Reference: Harper's — Deoxyribose.

questions.questionText: Glycogen branching enzyme (GBE) deficiency causes Andersen disease (GSD type IV). The pathological consequence of unbranched glycogen is:
questions.optionA: Abnormal, amylopectin-like glycogen with long linear chains is insoluble and resembles a foreign body → triggers progressive hepatic fibrosis and cirrhosis, with liver failure typically by age 3-5 years
questions.explanation: Andersen disease (GSD IV, branching enzyme deficiency): GBE normally creates α(1→6) branch points every 8-12 residues. Without GBE: glycogen has very long, unbranched chains → resembles AMYLOPECTIN (plant starch) → insoluble, poorly structured → treated as FOREIGN BODY by immune system → hepatocytes → progressive fibrosis and CIRRHOSIS. Unlike other GSDs: NOT primarily a storage problem but a STRUCTURAL/inflammatory problem. Presentation: failure to thrive, hepatosplenomegaly, progressive liver failure by age 3-5. Some forms: neuromuscular (adult polyglucosan body disease). Diagnosis: abnormal glycogen structure (long outer chains) on liver biopsy + absent branching enzyme. Treatment: liver transplantation. Contrast: GSD III (debranching enzyme) → glycogen with very short outer chains. Reference: Harper's — Andersen Disease.
questions.conceptTag: Andersen disease GBE unbranched glycogen cirrhosis

questions.questionText: Lectins are carbohydrate-binding proteins used extensively in clinical biochemistry. Concanavalin A (Con A) specifically binds:
questions.optionD: α-D-mannose and α-D-glucose residues on glycoproteins — used to study cell surface glycoproteins, isolate glycoproteins by affinity chromatography, and as a mitogen for T-lymphocyte activation
questions.explanation: Lectins — carbohydrate recognition in medicine: Lectins bind specific sugar structures with high specificity (like antibodies for proteins). Key lectins: Con A (concanavalin A, from jack bean): binds α-mannose/α-glucose → T-cell mitogen, glycoprotein purification. WGA (wheat germ agglutinin): binds GlcNAc, sialic acid → RBC agglutination studies. PHA (phytohemagglutinin): binds GlcNAc → T-cell mitogen (used in karyotyping). PNA (peanut agglutinin): binds Gal-β(1→3)-GalNAc → cancer biomarker (T/Tn antigens exposed on tumor cells). Ricin (Ricinus communis): binds galactose → TOXIC (inhibits ribosomes, potential bioweapon). Selectins: endogenous lectins (E, P, L-selectin → bind sialyl Lewis X). Clinical uses: blood typing, lymphocyte stimulation for chromosomal analysis, affinity purification. Reference: Harper's — Lectins.

questions.questionText: Sialic acid (N-acetylneuraminic acid, NANA) is important because it:
questions.optionA: Confers negative charge to cell surfaces and glycoproteins, involved in cell recognition
questions.explanation: Sialic acid (NANA): a 9-carbon acidic sugar found at terminal positions of oligosaccharide chains in glycoproteins and gangliosides (glycolipids). It confers negative charge to cell surfaces (contributes to glycocalyx), protects proteins from degradation, involved in cell-cell recognition and immune function. RBC surface sialic acid confers the negative charge. Neuraminidase (in influenza virus) cleaves sialic acid → virus release (oseltamivir inhibits this). Reference: Harper's — Sialic Acid.

questions.questionText: Essential pentosuria is a benign condition caused by deficiency of:
questions.explanation: Essential pentosuria: benign AR condition due to deficiency of L-xylulose reductase (NADPH-dependent) in the glucuronic acid pathway (uronic acid pathway). L-xylulose accumulates and is excreted in urine. Clinically insignificant — must be differentiated from diabetes mellitus (xylulose is a reducing sugar → positive Benedict's, but NOT glucose on specific glucose oxidase test). Primarily seen in Ashkenazi Jewish population. Reference: Harper's — Pentosuria.

questions.questionText: Blood group antigens (ABO) are determined by:
questions.optionA: Terminal sugar residues on glycoproteins/glycolipids on RBC surface
questions.explanation: ABO antigens: oligosaccharide chains on RBC glycoproteins/glycolipids. H antigen (precursor): L-fucose added to type 1/2 chain (H gene/FUT1). A antigen: N-acetylgalactosamine added to H (A-transferase). B antigen: D-galactose added to H (B-transferase). O: no additional sugar (only H). Bombay phenotype (hh): no H gene → no H antigen → can't make A or B → anti-A, anti-B, AND anti-H antibodies. Reference: Harper's — Blood Group Biochemistry.

questions.questionText: Invert sugar is formed by hydrolysis of:
questions.optionD: Sucrose (yielding glucose + fructose mixture which is levorotatory)
questions.explanation: Invert sugar: equimolar mixture of glucose and fructose obtained by hydrolysis of sucrose by invertase (sucrase) or dilute acid. Called 'invert' because sucrose is dextrorotatory (+66.5°), but after hydrolysis the mixture is levorotatory (-20°) due to fructose being more strongly levorotatory (-92°) than glucose is dextrorotatory (+52.5°). Net optical rotation reverses (inverts). Honey is a natural invert sugar. Reference: Harper's — Invert Sugar.

questions.questionText: Fructose is absorbed in the intestine by:
questions.explanation: Fructose absorption: GLUT5 (apical membrane of enterocytes, facilitated diffusion — sodium-independent, low-capacity). Exits basolaterally via GLUT2. Glucose and galactose: absorbed by SGLT1 (Na+-dependent secondary active transport, apical) and exit via GLUT2 (basolateral). Fructose absorption is slower and limited compared to glucose. Excess fructose ingestion → fructose malabsorption → osmotic diarrhea. Reference: Harper's — Sugar Absorption.

questions.questionText: N-acetylneuraminic acid (NANA/sialic acid) caps the terminal ends of glycoprotein oligosaccharides. Its removal by neuraminidase has which clinical consequence?
questions.optionD: Exposed galactose residues are recognized by asialoglycoprotein receptors on hepatocytes → rapid clearance of desialylated proteins from circulation; influenza neuraminidase cleaves sialic acid from host cell surfaces to release new virions
questions.explanation: Sialic acid — biological roles: (1) NEGATIVE CHARGE: sialic acid (NANA) adds negative charge to cell surface → repulsion between cells (prevents aggregation). RBC sialic acid → prevents rouleaux. (2) PROTEIN HALF-LIFE: terminal sialic acid on glycoproteins SHIELDS underlying galactose. Asialoglycoprotein receptor (liver, Ashwell-Morell receptor): recognizes exposed GALACTOSE on desialylated glycoproteins → endocytosis → lysosomal degradation. Explains why EPO, FSH, etc. are glycosylated with sialic acid → extends half-life. (3) INFLUENZA: hemagglutinin (HA) binds sialic acid for entry; NEURAMINIDASE (NA) cleaves sialic acid for viral release. Oseltamivir/zanamivir: neuraminidase INHIBITORS → block release. (4) Cancer: altered sialylation patterns on tumor cells. Reference: Harper's — Sialic Acid.

questions.questionText: Glycogen storage disease Type I (Von Gierke disease) is caused by deficiency of:
questions.explanation: Von Gierke (GSD Type I): glucose-6-phosphatase deficiency (liver, kidney). Cannot convert G6P → free glucose → severe fasting hypoglycemia, hepatomegaly (glycogen and fat accumulation), lactic acidosis, hyperuricemia, hyperlipidemia. GSD II (Pompe): lysosomal acid maltase → cardiomegaly. GSD III (Cori): debranching enzyme. GSD V (McArdle): muscle phosphorylase → exercise intolerance. GSD IV (Andersen): branching enzyme. Reference: Harper's — GSDs.

questions.questionText: The Osazone test produces characteristic crystal shape. Glucose, fructose, and mannose form:
questions.optionD: The same osazone (glucosazone) because they share C3-C6 configuration
questions.explanation: Osazone formation: reacts at C1 and C2 carbons → destroys stereoisomerism at C1 and C2. Since glucose, fructose, and mannose differ only at C1 and/or C2, they form the SAME osazone (glucosazone — needle-shaped crystals). Galactosazone has different morphology (rhombic plates). Lactosazone: cotton ball shape. Maltosazone: sunflower shape. Osazone reaction uses phenylhydrazine (3 moles). Reference: Harper's — Osazone.

questions.questionText: N-linked glycosylation of proteins begins in the:
questions.optionA: Endoplasmic reticulum (dolichol phosphate as lipid carrier)
questions.explanation: N-linked glycosylation: oligosaccharide chain assembled on dolichol phosphate (lipid carrier in ER membrane) → transferred en bloc to asparagine (Asn) residue in the sequence Asn-X-Ser/Thr (sequon) by oligosaccharyltransferase → processed in ER and Golgi (trimming and addition of sugars). O-linked glycosylation occurs in the Golgi (sugars added one at a time to Ser/Thr). Congenital disorders of glycosylation (CDG) → multisystem disease. Reference: Harper's — Glycosylation.

questions.questionText: In mutarotation of D-glucose, alpha and beta forms differ at:
questions.explanation: Correct answer: C. Alpha and beta glucose are anomers differing at C1 (anomeric carbon). Interconversion in solution via open-chain form leads to mutarotation. C2/C4 differences represent epimeric relations, not anomerism.

questions.questionText: Which statement is most accurate regarding sucrose hydrolysis?
questions.optionA: It yields glucose and fructose and the mixture is called invert sugar
questions.optionB: It cannot be hydrolyzed by intestinal enzymes
questions.optionD: It yields two molecules of glucose
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. Sucrose hydrolysis (sucrase/invertase or acid) yields glucose + fructose, called invert sugar. Fructose contributes high sweetness, so sweetness does not reduce. Sucrose is readily hydrolyzed in intestine.

questions.questionText: A reducing urine sample is Benedict-positive but glucose oxidase-negative in an infant with cataracts. Most likely diagnosis is:
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. Galactose and its metabolites can produce reducing sugar positivity on Benedict test while glucose oxidase remains negative for glucose. Infantile cataracts and liver dysfunction strongly favor galactosemia.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is correctly matched for blood group carbohydrate chemistry?
questions.optionA: H antigen precursor must be present for A or B expression
questions.explanation: Correct answer: C. H antigen is the precursor substrate for A and B transferases. A adds N-acetylgalactosamine; B adds galactose. O group retains unmodified H antigen.

questions.questionText: The only GAG that is non-sulfated and also serves as a backbone for large proteoglycan aggregates is:
questions.explanation: Correct answer: C. Hyaluronic acid is non-sulfated and forms large hydrated ECM backbones interacting with proteoglycan monomers via link proteins. Other listed GAGs are sulfated.

questions.questionText: Which pair is correctly matched with glycosidic linkage?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: C. Cellulose has beta(1->4) glucose linkages. Lactose is beta(1->4) galactose-glucose, maltose is alpha(1->4) glucose-glucose, and sucrose has alpha(1->2)beta linkage.

questions.questionText: Which clinical use best corresponds to mannitol, a sugar alcohol derivative?
questions.optionC: Osmotic diuretic used to reduce intracranial pressure
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. Mannitol is a sugar alcohol used as an osmotic diuretic in cerebral edema and acute glaucoma. It is not an insulin secretagogue or sucrase inhibitor.

questions.questionText: Most appropriate statement about glycogen vs amylopectin branching is:
questions.optionC: Glycogen is less branched than amylopectin
questions.optionD: Glycogen is more frequently branched than amylopectin
questions.explanation: Correct answer: C. Glycogen has branch points roughly every 8-12 residues, while amylopectin branches less frequently (about 24-30 residues). This supports rapid mobilization in animals.

questions.questionText: Seliwanoff's test gives a positive cherry-red color specifically with:
questions.optionC: Ketoses such as fructose (reacts faster with resorcinol in HCl)
questions.explanation: Seliwanoff's test distinguishes ketoses from aldoses. Ketoses (fructose) dehydrate faster in hot HCl → hydroxymethylfurfural → reacts with resorcinol → cherry-red color within 1-2 minutes. Aldoses react slowly (>5 min). Bial's test (orcinol + HCl + FeCl3) detects pentoses → green color. Barfoed's test distinguishes monosaccharides (react faster) from disaccharides. Reference: Harper's — Carbohydrate Qualitative Tests.

questions.questionText: I-cell disease (mucolipidosis II) is caused by deficiency of:
questions.optionA: N-acetylglucosamine-1-phosphotransferase (GlcNAc phosphotransferase) — failure to add mannose-6-phosphate tag
questions.explanation: I-cell disease: deficiency of GlcNAc phosphotransferase → lysosomal enzymes are NOT tagged with mannose-6-phosphate (M6P) → enzymes are secreted extracellularly instead of being directed to lysosomes → 'inclusion bodies' in fibroblasts (dense inclusions of undigested substrates). Severe phenotype resembles Hurler syndrome but involves MULTIPLE lysosomal enzyme deficiencies. Elevated lysosomal enzymes in plasma. Fatal in childhood. Reference: Harper's — Lysosomal Targeting.

questions.questionText: Trehalose is a non-reducing disaccharide composed of:
questions.optionC: Two glucose molecules linked α1→α1 (both anomeric carbons involved)
questions.explanation: Trehalose = glucose α1→α1 glucose. Both anomeric carbons are involved in the glycosidic bond → non-reducing sugar (like sucrose). Found in fungi, insects (hemolymph sugar), yeast. Acts as a stress protectant (stabilizes proteins and membranes during desiccation). Mycobacterial cord factor (trehalose dimycolate) is a virulence factor. Reference: Harper's — Disaccharides.

questions.questionText: SGLT1 transporter differs from GLUT2 in that SGLT1:
questions.optionC: Is sodium-independent facilitated diffusion on all cell types
questions.optionD: Is a secondary active transporter coupling glucose/galactose uptake with Na+ on the apical intestinal membrane
questions.explanation: SGLT1: Na+-glucose co-transporter (secondary active transport) on the apical brush border of intestinal enterocytes and proximal renal tubule. Transports glucose and galactose (NOT fructose — uses GLUT5). GLUT2: facilitated diffusion transporter on basolateral membrane of enterocytes and hepatocytes — bidirectional, high capacity. GLUT4: insulin-dependent (muscle/adipose). SGLT2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin) used in diabetes. Reference: Harper's — Sugar Transporters.

questions.questionText: Hunter syndrome (MPS II) differs from all other mucopolysaccharidoses in that it is:
questions.optionB: The mildest form with no skeletal involvement
questions.explanation: Hunter syndrome (MPS II): the ONLY X-linked MPS. All others are autosomal recessive. Deficient enzyme: iduronate-2-sulfatase → accumulation of dermatan sulfate and heparan sulfate. Features: similar to Hurler but MILDER, NO corneal clouding (distinguishing feature from Hurler), coarse facies, hepatosplenomegaly, skeletal dysplasia, progressive neurological decline (severe form). Hurler (MPS IH): α-L-iduronidase deficiency, AR, corneal clouding present. Reference: Harper's — MPS.

questions.questionText: N-acetylneuraminic acid (sialic acid) is a derivative of:
questions.explanation: Sialic acid (NANA) is a 9-carbon derivative of neuraminic acid, synthesized from N-acetylmannosamine + PEP. It occupies terminal positions of oligosaccharide chains on glycoproteins and gangliosides. Removal by neuraminidase (sialidase) exposes underlying galactose, marking glycoproteins for hepatic clearance. Influenza virus neuraminidase (NA) cleaves sialic acid for viral release → target of oseltamivir and zanamivir. Reference: Harper's — Sialic Acid Biosynthesis.

questions.questionText: Heparin differs from other GAGs in that it is:
questions.optionB: The only GAG without uronic acid
questions.optionD: An intracellular GAG found in mast cell granules with potent anticoagulant activity
questions.explanation: Heparin: unique GAG — stored intracellularly in mast cell granules (NOT in ECM like other GAGs). Most highly sulfated GAG (strongest negative charge). Potent anticoagulant: binds antithrombin III → enhances inactivation of thrombin and factor Xa by >1000-fold. Contains both glucuronic and iduronic acid. Heparan sulfate (extracellular, on cell surfaces) is structurally related but less sulfated. Low-molecular-weight heparins (enoxaparin) preferentially inhibit factor Xa. Reference: Harper's — Heparin.

questions.questionText: Keratan sulfate differs from other GAGs in that it:
questions.optionA: Contains galactose instead of uronic acid in its repeating disaccharide
questions.explanation: Keratan sulfate: the only GAG that does NOT contain a uronic acid. Its repeating unit is N-acetylglucosamine + galactose (instead of uronic acid). Found in cornea (type I) and cartilage/bone (type II). Morquio syndrome (MPS IV): deficiency of N-acetylgalactosamine-6-sulfatase (Type A) or β-galactosidase (Type B) → keratan sulfate accumulation → severe skeletal dysplasia, corneal clouding, odontoid hypoplasia. Reference: Harper's — GAG Structure.

questions.questionText: A newborn presents with hepatomegaly, cataracts, and E. coli sepsis after starting milk feeds. Urine shows a reducing substance that is NOT glucose. The most likely enzyme deficiency is:
questions.explanation: Classical galactosemia: Gal-1-P uridylyltransferase deficiency. Galactose-1-phosphate accumulates → toxic to liver (hepatomegaly, jaundice), lens (cataracts via galactitol accumulation by aldose reductase), brain (intellectual disability), kidneys. E. coli sepsis in neonates is a classic association. Urine: positive Benedict's (galactose is reducing), negative glucose oxidase test. Treatment: lifelong galactose-free diet. Reference: Harper's — Galactosemia.

questions.questionText: In Sly syndrome (MPS VII), the deficient enzyme is β-glucuronidase. It differs from other MPS disorders in that:
questions.optionB: It does not cause skeletal abnormalities
questions.optionC: It does not involve GAG accumulation
questions.optionD: It causes accumulation of dermatan, heparan AND chondroitin sulfate (substrates of β-glucuronidase span multiple GAG types), making it unique among MPS as affecting the broadest range of GAGs, and it can present as non-immune hydrops fetalis
questions.explanation: MPS VII (Sly syndrome): β-glucuronidase deficiency → accumulation of dermatan sulfate, heparan sulfate, AND chondroitin sulfate (broadest GAG accumulation of any MPS). Spectrum: severe (non-immune hydrops fetalis — prenatal presentation, one of the few IEMs presenting this way) to mild (survival to adulthood). Features: coarse facies, skeletal dysplasia, hepatosplenomegaly, intellectual disability, corneal clouding. Diagnosis: elevated urinary DS + HS + CS, enzyme assay. Treatment: ERT (vestronidase alfa, FDA approved 2017). MPS comparison: Hunter (II): HS + DS, X-linked, no corneal clouding. Hurler (IH): HS + DS, corneal clouding, AR. Sanfilippo (III): HS only, CNS dominant. Morquio (IV): KS + CS, skeletal dominant, normal intellect. Reference: Harper's — MPS VII.
questions.conceptTag: Sly syndrome MPS VII beta-glucuronidase hydrops

questions.questionText: Dextran is a branched polysaccharide composed of glucose units linked primarily by:
questions.optionA: α1→6 bonds in the main chain with α1→3 branch points
questions.explanation: Dextran: bacterial polysaccharide (produced by Leuconostoc, Streptococcus mutans). Main chain: α1→6 glucosidic bonds. Branches: α1→2, α1→3, or α1→4. Used clinically as a plasma volume expander (dextran 40, dextran 70). S. mutans produces dextran from sucrose → dental plaque → dental caries. Dextran is used in column chromatography (Sephadex). Reference: Harper's — Miscellaneous Polysaccharides.

questions.questionText: Glucuronic acid conjugation is impaired in neonatal jaundice primarily due to immaturity of:
questions.explanation: Neonatal (physiological) jaundice: immature UGT1A1 (UDP-glucuronosyltransferase) in liver → impaired conjugation of bilirubin with glucuronic acid → unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia. Bilirubin must be conjugated with 2 molecules of glucuronic acid (forming bilirubin diglucuronide) for excretion in bile. Crigler-Najjar Type I: complete absence of UGT1A1 → severe unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia, kernicterus. Type II: partial deficiency, responds to phenobarbital. Gilbert syndrome: mild UGT1A1 reduction. Reference: Harper's — Bilirubin Conjugation.

questions.questionText: In the context of carbohydrate analysis, the key difference between the Fehling/Benedict test and the enzymatic glucose oxidase method is:
questions.optionD: Benedict/Fehling detects ALL reducing sugars (glucose, galactose, fructose, lactose, maltose) via Cu²⁺ reduction, while glucose oxidase is SPECIFIC for β-D-glucose only — critical for diabetic monitoring to avoid false positives from other sugars
questions.explanation: Sugar detection methods — specificity: BENEDICT/FEHLING (chemical): Cu²⁺ (blue) → Cu⁺ (Cu₂O, brick-red) by ANY reducing sugar (free anomeric carbon). Detects: glucose, galactose, fructose, lactose, maltose, etc. NOT specific for glucose. Clinical issue: positive in galactosemia, lactosuria, not just DM. GLUCOSE OXIDASE (enzymatic): β-D-glucose + O₂ → D-glucono-δ-lactone + H₂O₂ (glucose oxidase). H₂O₂ + chromogen → color (peroxidase). HIGHLY SPECIFIC for β-D-GLUCOSE only. Used in: glucometers (diabetic monitoring), urine dipsticks. Does NOT detect galactose, fructose, etc. HEXOKINASE method (reference method): hexokinase phosphorylates glucose → G6P → G6PDH converts → NADPH (measured spectrophotometrically at 340nm). Most accurate. Reference: Harper's — Sugar Detection.

questions.questionText: Dermatan sulfate accumulates in which of the following mucopolysaccharidoses?
questions.optionA: Hurler syndrome (MPS IH) and Hunter syndrome (MPS II)
questions.explanation: Dermatan sulfate + heparan sulfate accumulate in both Hurler (MPS IH, α-L-iduronidase deficiency) and Hunter (MPS II, iduronate-2-sulfatase deficiency). Sanfilippo (MPS III): heparan sulfate only. Morquio (MPS IV): keratan sulfate. Maroteaux-Lamy (MPS VI): dermatan sulfate (arylsulfatase B/ARSB deficiency). Sly (MPS VII): dermatan sulfate + heparan sulfate + chondroitin sulfate (β-glucuronidase deficiency). Reference: Harper's — MPS Classification.

questions.questionText: O-linked glycosylation differs from N-linked glycosylation in that O-linked glycosylation:
questions.optionA: Involves en bloc transfer of 14-sugar oligosaccharide
questions.optionB: Uses dolichol phosphate as a lipid carrier and occurs in ER
questions.optionC: Occurs in the Golgi apparatus with sugars added one at a time to serine or threonine residues
questions.explanation: O-linked glycosylation: occurs in Golgi, sugars added one at a time (no dolichol carrier) to hydroxyl group of serine or threonine. N-linked: begins in ER, uses dolichol phosphate, en bloc transfer of pre-assembled 14-sugar (Glc3Man9GlcNAc2) oligosaccharide to Asn in Asn-X-Ser/Thr sequon. O-linked examples: mucins, blood group antigens, proteoglycans (GAG chains attached O-linked to serine). Tunicamycin inhibits N-linked glycosylation (blocks dolichol-PP-GlcNAc synthesis). Reference: Harper's — Glycosylation Types.

questions.questionText: Trehalose (α,α-1,1-glycosidic bond) functions as a stress protectant in organisms like yeast and tardigrades because:
questions.optionB: It is not metabolized by any organism
questions.optionC: It provides more energy than glucose
questions.optionD: Trehalose forms a glass-like matrix around cellular structures during desiccation, replacing water molecules around proteins and membranes through hydrogen bonding — preserving their native conformation during extreme dehydration
questions.explanation: Trehalose — bioprotectant mechanism: Non-reducing disaccharide (both anomeric carbons involved in bond → very stable). Bioprotection: (1) VITRIFICATION: during dehydration, trehalose forms an amorphous GLASS-LIKE matrix → immobilizes biological molecules in their native state, (2) WATER REPLACEMENT: trehalose OH groups substitute for water molecules in H-bonding with protein surfaces and phospholipid head groups, (3) Prevents protein aggregation and membrane fusion. Found in: yeast, fungi, insects, tardigrades (survive extreme desiccation, heat, radiation). Medical applications: trehalose used in preservation of vaccines, enzymes, stem cells, organ transplant solutions, lyophilized products. Trehalase: brush border enzyme (small intestine) hydrolyzes trehalose. Trehalase deficiency: trehalose intolerance (diarrhea after eating mushrooms — rich in trehalose). Reference: Harper's — Trehalose.

questions.questionText: The polyol (sorbitol) pathway is clinically significant in diabetes because excess glucose is converted to sorbitol by:
questions.explanation: Polyol pathway: Glucose → sorbitol (by aldose reductase, using NADPH) → fructose (by sorbitol dehydrogenase, using NAD+). In hyperglycemia/diabetes: excess glucose enters polyol pathway in insulin-independent tissues (lens, retina, nerve, kidney) → sorbitol accumulates (cannot cross cell membrane easily) → osmotic damage → diabetic cataracts, peripheral neuropathy, retinopathy, nephropathy. Aldose reductase inhibitors (epalrestat) may help. Same mechanism causes cataracts in galactosemia (galactitol). Reference: Harper's — Polyol Pathway.

questions.questionText: Resistant starch differs from regular starch in clinical nutrition because:
questions.optionA: It is absorbed faster than regular starch
questions.optionB: It has the same metabolic fate as digestible starch
questions.optionC: Resistant starch escapes digestion in the small intestine and is fermented by colonic bacteria into short-chain fatty acids (butyrate, propionate, acetate), which provide colonocyte nutrition and have anti-inflammatory properties
questions.optionD: It is completely indigestible and has no metabolic value
questions.explanation: Resistant starch (RS): starch that resists α-amylase digestion in the small intestine. Types: RS1 (physically inaccessible — whole grains), RS2 (native granular — raw potato, green banana), RS3 (retrograded — cooled cooked starch), RS4 (chemically modified). Reaches COLON → fermented by microbiota → SHORT-CHAIN FATTY ACIDS (SCFAs): Butyrate: preferred fuel for COLONOCYTES, promotes differentiation, may protect against colorectal cancer. Has HDAC inhibitor activity. Propionate: hepatic gluconeogenesis regulation, cholesterol-lowering effects. Acetate: peripheral energy source. Health benefits: improved insulin sensitivity, reduced postprandial glucose spikes, prebiotic effect, increased satiety. Glycemic index: resistant starch lowers the glycemic index of foods. Reference: Harper's — Resistant Starch.

questions.questionText: Lactose synthase in the mammary gland requires which modifier protein to become specific for lactose synthesis?
questions.optionD: α-Lactalbumin (regulatory subunit that modifies galactosyltransferase specificity)
questions.explanation: Lactose synthase = galactosyltransferase + α-lactalbumin. Galactosyltransferase alone: transfers galactose to N-acetylglucosamine (for glycoprotein synthesis). When α-lactalbumin (produced in mammary gland under prolactin stimulation during lactation) associates with galactosyltransferase, it changes substrate specificity → now transfers galactose to glucose → lactose synthesis. This is a classic example of enzyme regulation by a modifier protein. Reference: Harper's — Lactose Synthesis.

questions.questionText: A 4-year-old child with coarse facial features, skeletal abnormalities, hepatosplenomegaly, and corneal clouding is most likely to have which MPS subtype?
questions.explanation: Hurler syndrome (MPS IH): most severe MPS. α-L-iduronidase deficiency → dermatan + heparan sulfate accumulation. Features: coarse facial features (gargoylism), severe skeletal dysplasia (dysostosis multiplex), hepatosplenomegaly, corneal clouding, intellectual disability, upper airway obstruction, cardiac valve disease. Death usually by age 10. Hunter syndrome is similar BUT lacks corneal clouding and is X-linked. Sanfilippo: predominantly CNS involvement. Morquio: severe skeletal with preserved intellect. Reference: Harper's — Hurler Syndrome.

questions.questionText: I-cell disease (mucolipidosis II) results from deficiency of which enzyme involved in glycoprotein processing?
questions.optionC: GlcNAc-1-phosphotransferase — which adds mannose-6-phosphate (M6P) targeting signal to lysosomal enzyme oligosaccharides in the cis-Golgi
questions.explanation: I-cell disease (inclusion cell disease/mucolipidosis II): GlcNAc-1-phosphotransferase deficiency → CANNOT add M6P tag to N-linked oligosaccharides of lysosomal enzymes. Without M6P signal, lysosomal enzymes are NOT recognized by M6P receptors in trans-Golgi → secreted EXTRACELLULARLY instead of being directed to lysosomes. Result: lysosomes devoid of enzymes → undigested substrates accumulate as inclusion bodies. Plasma: ELEVATED levels of ALL lysosomal enzymes (diagnostic clue — enzymes are made but misdirected). Features: severe (Hurler-like), coarse facies, skeletal abnormalities, psychomotor retardation, restricted joint mobility. Death by age 5-8. M6P pathway: cis-Golgi adds M6P → trans-Golgi M6P receptors → clathrin-coated vesicles → endosomes → lysosomes. Reference: Harper's — I-cell Disease.

questions.questionText: ABO blood group antigens are determined by which type of sugar modification on RBC glycolipids/glycoproteins?
questions.optionD: Terminal sugar added to H antigen — N-acetylgalactosamine (GalNAc) by A-transferase for group A, galactose by B-transferase for group B, no addition for group O
questions.explanation: ABO blood group biochemistry: H antigen: fucose added to terminal galactose of glycolipid/glycoprotein on RBC surface by fucosyltransferase (H gene, chromosome 19). Group O: only H antigen (no further modification). Group A: A-transferase (chromosome 9) adds N-ACETYLGALACTOSAMINE (GalNAc) to H antigen. Group B: B-transferase adds GALACTOSE to H antigen. Group AB: both transferases present. Bombay phenotype (Oh): homozygous h/h → NO fucosyltransferase → NO H antigen → no A or B antigens possible → appears as 'O' but has anti-H antibodies (unlike normal O). Cannot receive ANY ABO blood — only Bombay blood. The A and B transferases differ by only 4 amino acids. Reference: Harper's — ABO Blood Group.

questions.questionText: Heparin functions as an anticoagulant through a specific biochemical mechanism. The key interaction is:
questions.optionA: Heparin (a GAG) binds to and activates ANTITHROMBIN III (AT-III), inducing a conformational change that increases AT-III's ability to inhibit thrombin and Factor Xa by ~1000-fold
questions.optionD: Direct inhibition of thrombin active site
questions.explanation: Heparin mechanism — GAG pharmacology: Heparin is a highly sulfated GAG (repeating units of glucuronate/iduronate + glucosamine, heavily sulfated). Mechanism: specific pentasaccharide sequence in heparin binds ANTITHROMBIN III (AT-III/SERPINC1, a serine protease inhibitor) → conformational change → AT-III inhibition of thrombin (Factor IIa), Factor Xa, and other coagulation proteases enhanced ~1000-fold. Unfractionated heparin (UFH): inhibits both thrombin AND Xa (requires bridging — heparin must bind both AT-III and thrombin simultaneously → needs ≥18 saccharide units). Low molecular weight heparin (LMWH — enoxaparin): primarily inhibits Factor Xa (shorter chains can still bind AT-III pentasaccharide but too short to bridge to thrombin). Fondaparinux: synthetic pentasaccharide → ONLY anti-Xa. Protamine sulfate reverses UFH. Reference: Harper's — Heparin Mechanism.

questions.questionText: HbA1c measures glycated hemoglobin. The biochemical reaction that forms HbA1c is:
questions.optionC: Non-enzymatic glycation — glucose binds to N-terminal valine of β-globin chain via Schiff base → Amadori rearrangement → stable ketoamine (reflects average blood glucose over 2-3 months)
questions.explanation: HbA1c — non-enzymatic glycation: Step 1: glucose reacts NON-enzymatically with free amino group (N-terminal valine of β-chain) → SCHIFF BASE (aldimine, reversible, labile). Step 2: AMADORI rearrangement → KETOAMINE (stable, irreversible) = HbA1c. This is proportional to average blood glucose concentration over RBC lifespan (~120 days, so reflects 2-3 months). Normal HbA1c: <5.7%. Pre-diabetes: 5.7-6.4%. Diabetes: ≥6.5%. Target for diabetics: <7% (ADA). Glycation vs glycosylation: GLYCATION = non-enzymatic (random, pathological). GLYCOSYLATION = enzymatic (specific, physiological — N-linked, O-linked). Advanced glycation end products (AGEs): prolonged glycation → cross-linked proteins → contribute to diabetic complications (nephropathy, retinopathy, neuropathy, atherosclerosis) via RAGE receptor activation. Reference: Harper's — HbA1c.

questions.questionText: Selectins are carbohydrate-binding proteins (lectins) that mediate the initial step of leukocyte adhesion to endothelium. The carbohydrate ligand they recognize is:
questions.optionD: Sialyl Lewis X (sLex) — a tetrasaccharide containing fucose and sialic acid on leukocyte/endothelial glycoproteins
questions.explanation: Selectins and leukocyte adhesion — carbohydrate recognition: Selectins: calcium-dependent lectins (carbohydrate-binding proteins). Three types: E-selectin (endothelial, induced by IL-1/TNF), P-selectin (platelets/endothelial, stored in Weibel-Palade bodies), L-selectin (leukocytes, constitutive). All recognize SIALYL LEWIS X (sLex): tetrasaccharide (Sialic acid-Gal-GlcNAc-Fuc) displayed on glycoproteins (e.g., PSGL-1/P-selectin glycoprotein ligand-1). Adhesion cascade: (1) ROLLING: selectin-sLex interactions (weak, transient), (2) ACTIVATION: chemokine signaling activates integrins on leukocyte, (3) FIRM ADHESION: activated integrins (e.g., LFA-1) bind endothelial ICAMs (immunoglobulin superfamily), (4) TRANSMIGRATION: diapedesis. Leukocyte adhesion deficiency type 2 (LAD-2): defective fucose metabolism → CANNOT synthesize sLex → impaired selectin binding → recurrent infections. Reference: Harper's — Selectins.
questions.conceptTag: Selectin sialyl Lewis X leukocyte adhesion

questions.questionText: Congenital disorders of glycosylation (CDG) are a group of multisystem diseases caused by defects in N-linked glycosylation. The most common type (CDG-Ia/PMM2-CDG) results from deficiency of:
questions.optionA: Phosphomannomutase 2 (PMM2) — which converts mannose-6-phosphate to mannose-1-phosphate, essential for GDP-mannose synthesis for N-glycan assembly
questions.explanation: CDG-Ia (PMM2-CDG): most common CDG (>800 types described). PMM2 converts mannose-6-P → mannose-1-P → GDP-mannose (donor for N-glycan assembly). Deficiency → impaired synthesis of lipid-linked oligosaccharide (LLO) precursor → hypoglycosylation of serum glycoproteins. Features: multisystem — intellectual disability, cerebellar hypoplasia (olivopontocerebellar atrophy), inverted nipples, abnormal fat pads, strabismus, liver dysfunction, coagulopathy (decreased anticoagulant proteins C, S, AT-III), cardiac involvement. Diagnosis: transferrin isoelectric focusing (IEF) shows increased disialotransferrin (type 1 pattern — underglycosylation). The inverted nipples + cerebellar hypoplasia combination is highly suggestive. No specific treatment — supportive care. Reference: Harper's — CDG-Ia.

questions.questionText: Hyaluronic acid differs from other GAGs because it:
questions.optionA: Is NOT sulfated, NOT covalently attached to a core protein, and is synthesized at the cell surface (not in the Golgi) — making it unique among GAGs
questions.explanation: Hyaluronic acid — unique GAG: All other GAGs (chondroitin sulfate, dermatan sulfate, keratan sulfate, heparan sulfate, heparin) are: (1) Sulfated, (2) Covalently linked to core protein (forming proteoglycans), (3) Synthesized in Golgi. Hyaluronic acid is UNIQUE: (1) NOT SULFATED, (2) NOT attached to core protein (free polysaccharide), (3) Synthesized at CELL SURFACE by hyaluronan synthase (HAS, integral membrane protein), (4) Very large (up to millions of Da — largest GAG), (5) Repeating disaccharide: GlcA-GlcNAc. Functions: absorbs water → viscous solutions (shock absorption in joints, vitreous humor), tissue hydration, cell migration during embryogenesis, wound healing. Hyaluronidase: enzyme that degrades hyaluronic acid (spreading factor in infections, venom). Used therapeutically: intra-articular viscosupplementation (osteoarthritis), dermal fillers. Reference: Harper's — Hyaluronic Acid.

questions.questionText: Galactosemia (classic type I) results from deficiency of galactose-1-phosphate uridylyltransferase (GALT). The toxic metabolites that accumulate include:
questions.optionB: Galactose-1-phosphate (inhibits multiple enzymes) and GALACTITOL (from aldose reductase — causes cataracts by osmotic swelling of lens fibers)
questions.explanation: Classic galactosemia (GALT deficiency): Pathway: Lactose (milk) → glucose + galactose → galactokinase → galactose-1-phosphate → GALT (blocked) → Gal-1-P + UDP-glucose ↔ G1P + UDP-galactose. Toxic accumulation: (1) Galactose-1-phosphate: inhibits phosphoglucomutase, G6P dehydrogenase → depletes UDP-glucose → impaired glycogen synthesis, inhibits multiple metabolic pathways. (2) GALACTITOL: galactose reduced by ALDOSE REDUCTASE → galactitol (sugar alcohol). Galactitol cannot be further metabolized → accumulates in LENS → osmotic gradient → water influx → lens fiber swelling → juvenile CATARACTS (same mechanism as sorbitol in diabetic cataracts). Features: neonatal jaundice, hepatomegaly, E. coli sepsis (classic association), intellectual disability, cataracts. Treatment: GALACTOSE-FREE diet (no lactose-containing milk products). Galactokinase deficiency: only cataracts (galactitol). Reference: Harper's — Galactosemia.

questions.questionText: Proteoglycans differ from glycoproteins in that proteoglycans:
questions.optionB: Have GAG chains constituting >95% of their mass (carbohydrate-dominant), while glycoproteins have shorter oligosaccharide chains constituting <60% of mass (protein-dominant)
questions.explanation: Proteoglycan vs glycoprotein — structural comparison: PROTEOGLYCANS: core protein + one or more GAG chains (long unbranched polysaccharides: chondroitin sulfate, heparan sulfate, keratan sulfate, dermatan sulfate). GAGs are >95% of mass → carbohydrate DOMINANT. GAG attachment: O-linked to serine via Gal-Gal-Xyl linker tetrasaccharide. Functions: water retention (due to negative charges on sulfate/carboxylate groups → attract cations → osmotic swelling), shock absorption, cell signaling, growth factor binding. Examples: aggrecan (cartilage), perlecan (basement membrane), syndecan (cell surface). GLYCOPROTEINS: protein dominant (<60% carbohydrate). Short oligosaccharides (N-linked to Asn, O-linked to Ser/Thr). Examples: antibodies, hormones (FSH, LH, TSH, hCG), mucins, blood group substances. Reference: Harper's — Proteoglycans vs Glycoproteins.

questions.questionText: The reducing sugar test (Benedict's test) is positive for glucose and galactose but NEGATIVE for sucrose because:
questions.optionB: In sucrose, BOTH anomeric carbons (C1 of glucose and C2 of fructose) are involved in the glycosidic bond → no free anomeric carbon available to open and reduce Cu²⁺
questions.optionC: Sucrose contains fructose which is non-reducing
questions.optionD: Sucrose is too large to react
questions.explanation: Reducing sugars — biochemical basis: A reducing sugar has a FREE (or potentially free) anomeric carbon that can open to the aldehyde/ketone form → reduce Cu²+ → Cu⁺ (Cu2O, brick-red precipitate in Benedict's). Glucose, galactose, lactose, maltose: one anomeric carbon is FREE → reducing. SUCROSE: α(1→2)β glycosidic bond — the bond involves the anomeric carbons of BOTH glucose (C1) AND fructose (C2). No free anomeric carbon → CANNOT open → NON-REDUCING. It's the only common non-reducing disaccharide. Trehalose: also non-reducing (α,α-1,1-glycosidic bond — both anomeric carbons involved). Clinical relevance: Benedict's test detects reducing sugars in urine (glycosuria). Positive for glucose (diabetes), galactose (galactosemia), lactose (lactase deficiency with lactouria). NOT positive for sucrose. Modern: glucose oxidase (specific for glucose) used in glucometers. Reference: Harper's — Reducing Sugars.

questions.questionText: A 10-month-old child presents with frequent episodes of weakness, sweating, and hepatomegaly. Fasting blood glucose is 40 g/dL. Administration of glucagon fails to increase blood glucose but significantly increases blood lactate levels. The deficient enzyme is:
questions.explanation: Von Gierke disease (GSD I) is characterized by a deficiency in glucose-6-phosphatase, preventing the final step of both glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis, leading to severe fasting hypoglycemia. Administration of glucagon stimulates glycogen breakdown, but because glucose-6-phosphate cannot be dephosphorylated, it is instead shunted into glycolysis, producing excess lactate and causing lactic acidosis.

questions.questionText: A student says, 'D-glucose is D because it is dextrorotatory.' Which statement is correct?
questions.optionB: False; D/L is based on configuration relative to glyceraldehyde, not optical rotation
questions.optionC: True only for monosaccharides, not disaccharides
questions.optionD: False; D means carbonyl at C1
questions.explanation: D/L denotes configuration at the reference chiral center, whereas dextrorotation/levorotation is an experimentally measured optical property. A is a classic exam trap. C is incorrect because the concept itself is independent of sugar class. D confuses D/L with aldose/ketose logic.

questions.questionText: Which pair is correctly matched?
questions.explanation: D-glucose and D-mannose differ only at C2, so they are epimers. A is wrong because D- and L-forms are enantiomers. B is wrong because glucose and galactose are C4 epimers, not anomers. D is wrong because they are not mirror-image stereoisomers of the same molecule.

questions.questionText: In beta-D-fructofuranose, the anomeric carbon is:
questions.explanation: Fructose is a ketose, so its carbonyl carbon is C2; this becomes the anomeric carbon in the ring form. C1 is anomeric for aldoses like glucose. C5 participates in ring closure but is not anomeric. C6 is terminal alcohol carbon.

questions.questionText: Which statement best distinguishes cyclic glucose from cyclic fructose?
questions.optionB: Glucose forms a hemiacetal; fructose forms a hemiketal
questions.optionC: Glucose forms ketal; fructose forms acetal
questions.explanation: Aldose cyclization yields hemiacetal; ketose cyclization yields hemiketal. A and B each apply to only one class. D incorrectly names full acetal/ketal products instead of cyclic monosaccharide hemi forms.

questions.questionText: About Haworth projection of D-sugars, the most accurate rule is:
questions.optionB: CH2OH at the reference carbon points down
questions.optionC: CH2OH at the reference carbon points up
questions.optionD: Anomeric OH up always means alpha
questions.explanation: In standard Haworth conventions for D-sugars, CH2OH is drawn above the ring. C is wrong because alpha/beta is relative to CH2OH (for D-sugars, alpha is opposite and beta is same side). D confuses ring orientation with carbonyl position.
questions.conceptTag: Haworth alpha beta D sugar rule

questions.questionText: Which set contains one deoxy sugar, one uronic acid, and one amino sugar in that exact order?
questions.explanation: A correctly maps derivative classes. B starts with a non-deoxy sugar and ends with a neutral sugar. C has no uronic acid in the middle. D ends with sugar alcohol rather than amino sugar.

questions.questionText: Lactose versus sucrose: which comparison is correct?
questions.optionA: Lactose is reducing; sucrose is non-reducing
questions.optionD: Both have free anomeric carbon on each monosaccharide
questions.explanation: Lactose retains one free anomeric carbon and is reducing. Sucrose links both anomeric carbons (alpha1->beta2), making it non-reducing. A and B ignore this core structural difference. D gives the wrong linkage for sucrose.

questions.questionText: Which disaccharide is non-reducing specifically because both anomeric carbons are involved in an alpha,alpha-1,1 linkage?
questions.explanation: Trehalose is alpha,alpha-1,1-linked glucose disaccharide with no free anomeric carbon. Maltose, lactose, and isomaltose each have one free anomeric carbon and are reducing.

questions.questionText: Which statement about glycogen, amylopectin, and cellulose is correct?
questions.optionA: Cellulose has alpha(1->4) linkage and is digestible by humans
questions.optionB: Amylopectin is more frequently branched than glycogen
questions.optionC: Glycogen has more non-reducing ends than amylopectin per equal chain length
questions.explanation: Glycogen is more densely branched than amylopectin, producing more non-reducing ends for rapid mobilization. A is wrong because cellulose is beta(1->4) and indigestible to humans. B reverses branch frequency. D is wrong since glycogen and amylopectin are branched.

questions.questionText: Which pair is correctly matched?
questions.explanation: Dextran is a bacterial alpha(1->6)-dominant branched glucan. Inulin is not digested by human amylase. Cellulose is a structural plant polymer. Glycogen is alpha(1->4) with alpha(1->6) branches, not beta(1->4).

questions.questionText: A sugar solution gives rapid positive Barfoed test, positive Benedict test, and cherry-red Seliwanoff within 2 minutes. Most likely sugar:
questions.explanation: Rapid Barfoed positivity indicates monosaccharide, and rapid Seliwanoff cherry-red indicates ketose. Fructose fits both. Glucose is aldose. Lactose and maltose are disaccharides with slower or negative Barfoed under standard timing.

questions.questionText: Urine is Benedict positive but glucose oxidase strip negative in an asymptomatic adult after fruit-heavy diet. Most likely diagnosis:
questions.explanation: Essential fructosuria (fructokinase deficiency) is benign and causes urinary fructose, a reducing sugar detected by Benedict but not by glucose oxidase. HFI causes symptomatic hypoglycemia/hepatic toxicity. Diabetes gives glucose oxidase positivity. Classical galactosemia presents in infancy after milk feeds.
questions.conceptTag: Benedict positive glucose oxidase negative fructose

questions.questionText: Which test is most specific for pentoses (e.g., ribose, xylose)?
questions.explanation: Bial test is a pentose-focused color reaction. Seliwanoff differentiates ketose versus aldose. Fehling detects reducing sugars broadly. Osazone helps pattern recognition but is not pentose-specific.

questions.questionText: Which GAG-location pair is correctly matched?
questions.optionA: Hyaluronic acid - mast-cell granules as main anticoagulant
questions.optionB: Heparan sulfate - basement membrane and cell surface
questions.optionD: Chondroitin sulfate - principal RBC membrane GAG
questions.explanation: Heparan sulfate is abundant in basement membranes and cell surfaces. A actually describes heparin. C is wrong because keratan sulfate is important in cornea. D is wrong because chondroitin sulfate is ECM/cartilage dominant.

questions.questionText: A boy has coarse facies, hepatosplenomegaly, joint stiffness, and no corneal clouding. Likely diagnosis:
questions.explanation: Hunter syndrome (MPS II) classically has no corneal clouding and is X-linked. Hurler typically has corneal clouding. Morquio is predominantly skeletal with relatively preserved intellect. Maroteaux-Lamy is AR and usually has corneal clouding.

questions.questionText: A child has coarse facies and severe developmental delay; plasma lysosomal enzymes are elevated, but fibroblasts show inclusion bodies. Primary defect:
questions.optionB: Failure of mannose-6-phosphate tagging in cis-Golgi
questions.explanation: This is I-cell disease due to defective M6P tagging, causing lysosomal enzymes to be secreted instead of targeted to lysosomes. A and B are specific MPS enzyme defects. D does not explain pan-lysosomal enzyme elevation in plasma plus inclusion bodies.

questions.questionText: A transfusion-dependent patient types as O but reacts with all O donor blood. Most likely biochemical basis:
questions.optionC: Absence of H antigen due to FUT1 deficiency (Bombay phenotype)
questions.optionD: Excess sialic acid masking B antigen
questions.explanation: Bombay phenotype lacks H antigen, so A/B antigens cannot be built and anti-H antibodies react even with standard O blood. A and B cannot explain anti-H incompatibility. D is not the mechanism of Bombay phenotype.

questions.questionText: Infant develops vomiting, lethargy, hypoglycemia, and hepatomegaly after fruit juice introduction. Most likely deficient enzyme:
questions.explanation: Hereditary fructose intolerance is due to aldolase B deficiency, causing fructose-1-phosphate trapping, phosphate depletion, and severe hepatic symptoms after fructose/sucrose intake. Fructokinase deficiency is usually benign essential fructosuria. Galactokinase and GALT defects are galactose disorders.
questions.conceptTag: HFI aldolase B vs essential fructosuria

questions.questionText: All of the following about N-linked and O-linked glycosylation are true, except:
questions.optionA: N-linked attachment is to Asn in Asn-X-Ser/Thr motif
questions.optionB: O-linked glycosylation starts in ER with en bloc transfer
questions.optionC: Mannose-6-phosphate is required for lysosomal enzyme targeting
questions.optionD: N-linked transfer uses a dolichol-linked oligosaccharide precursor
questions.explanation: O-linked glycosylation occurs mainly in Golgi by stepwise sugar addition, not ER en bloc transfer. A and B are core N-linked features. D is correct for lysosomal enzyme sorting.

questions.questionText: Which statement best integrates lectin and sialic acid biology?
questions.optionA: Peanut agglutinin binds only mannose-rich N-glycans
questions.optionB: Neuraminidase increases terminal sialic acid, prolonging glycoprotein half-life
questions.optionC: Removal of terminal sialic acid exposes galactose and accelerates hepatic clearance via asialoglycoprotein receptor
questions.optionD: Concanavalin A binds terminal sialic acid with highest affinity
questions.explanation: Desialylation exposes terminal galactose, promoting hepatic asialoglycoprotein receptor-mediated clearance. A is opposite (neuraminidase removes sialic acid). C is wrong because Con A favors alpha-mannose/alpha-glucose motifs. D is wrong because PNA recognizes Gal-beta(1->3)-GalNAc motifs.

questions.questionText: Which representation gives the most realistic conformational detail for beta-D-glucopyranose in solution?
questions.optionB: Chair conformation (predominantly 4C1 with substituents mostly equatorial)
questions.explanation: Chair conformation best captures steric strain and substituent orientation. beta-D-glucopyranose is most stable in 4C1 chair because bulky groups are largely equatorial. Fischer and Haworth are useful schematic conventions but less conformationally accurate. Open-chain form is minor at equilibrium.

questions.questionText: Which statement about pyranose versus furanose forms is correct?
questions.optionB: Glucose exists mainly as furanose in aqueous solution
questions.optionC: Fructose can exist as both furanose and pyranose forms in solution
questions.optionD: Pyranose rings always contain five atoms
questions.explanation: Fructose interconverts between furanose and pyranose rings in solution. Glucose predominantly exists as pyranose (six-membered ring). Pyranose is six-membered and furanose is five-membered. Ring size does not by itself determine reducing property; free anomeric carbon does.

questions.questionText: Which sugar is correctly classified as a ketohexose?
questions.explanation: Fructose is a ketose with six carbons (ketohexose). Glucose and galactose are aldohexoses; ribose is an aldopentose. This is a common aldose-ketose and pentose-hexose classification trap.

questions.questionText: A carbohydrate gives silver mirror with Tollens reagent but is negative on glucose oxidase strip. Most likely interpretation is:
questions.optionA: Test indicates ketone body in urine
questions.optionC: A non-glucose reducing sugar is present
questions.explanation: Tollens detects reducing sugars chemically, while glucose oxidase is glucose-specific. Therefore this pattern suggests a reducing sugar other than glucose (for example galactose or fructose). Sucrose is non-reducing unless hydrolyzed.

questions.questionText: In standard practical conditions, Barfoed test helps differentiate monosaccharides from disaccharides because:
questions.optionA: Only ketoses react in acidic medium
questions.optionB: Monosaccharides reduce copper acetate more rapidly in acidic medium
questions.explanation: Barfoed reagent is acidic; monosaccharides reduce copper ions quickly, while disaccharides react slowly or weakly within test time. A is false because this is not ketose-specific. C is false because some disaccharides are reducing. D is incorrect.

questions.questionText: A child with profound neurodegeneration, behavioral disturbance, and relatively mild skeletal changes has accumulation predominantly of heparan sulfate. Most likely diagnosis:
questions.explanation: Sanfilippo syndrome is the MPS classically dominated by CNS and behavioral deterioration with heparan sulfate accumulation. Morquio is mainly skeletal with preserved intellect. Maroteaux-Lamy has dermatan sulfate accumulation and no major CNS decline. Sly has broader GAG accumulation.

questions.questionText: Which MPS is most accurately characterized by severe skeletal dysplasia with relatively preserved intelligence and keratan sulfate accumulation?
questions.explanation: Morquio syndrome (MPS IV) is skeletal-predominant and associated with keratan sulfate accumulation, with relatively preserved intelligence. Hurler/Hunter have broader multisystem involvement; Sanfilippo is CNS-predominant.

questions.questionText: Which enzyme deficiency is classically associated with Maroteaux-Lamy syndrome (MPS VI)?
questions.explanation: MPS VI (Maroteaux-Lamy) is due to arylsulfatase B deficiency with dermatan sulfate accumulation. Iduronate-2-sulfatase is Hunter (MPS II), alpha-L-iduronidase is Hurler (MPS I), and beta-glucuronidase is Sly (MPS VII).

questions.questionText: All are true about heparin and heparan sulfate, except:
questions.optionA: Heparan sulfate is prominent in basement membrane and cell surface proteoglycans
questions.optionB: Heparan sulfate is the principal therapeutic anticoagulant used clinically
questions.optionC: Heparin is generally more highly sulfated than heparan sulfate
questions.optionD: Heparin is typically stored in mast-cell granules
questions.explanation: Therapeutic anticoagulant is heparin, not heparan sulfate. A-C are true distinctions. This is a frequent comparison trap in GAG questions.

questions.questionText: Which statement regarding mucins is most accurate?
questions.optionA: They are lysosomal enzymes tagged by mannose-6-phosphate
questions.optionB: They are structural GAG polymers without core protein
questions.optionC: They are predominantly N-linked glycoconjugates with minimal carbohydrate
questions.optionD: They are rich in O-linked oligosaccharides attached to serine/threonine and contribute to viscoelastic protective secretions
questions.explanation: Mucins are heavily O-glycosylated glycoproteins that protect epithelial surfaces and provide viscosity. A is incorrect profile, C describes lysosomal targeting pathway, and D describes free GAG logic like hyaluronan rather than mucins.

questions.questionText: A child has abdominal pain and bloating after milk but tolerates fruit juices; stool is acidic with reducing substances after lactose load. Most likely defect is:
questions.explanation: Lactase deficiency causes lactose malabsorption with fermentation, acidic stool, gas, and osmotic diarrhea. Fruit tolerance argues against fructose pathway disorders. Sucrase-isomaltase deficiency would involve sucrose/starch intolerance pattern.
